one_line_takeaway: LampMark ties a face image's watermark to its own PCA-reduced facial-landmark geometry (encrypted via cellular-automaton Rule 30) so that Deepfake edits break watermark-content consistency, yielding 98.4-98.6% mixed detection AUC and ~87% watermark recovery under seven Deepfake manipulations while passive detectors fall below 70%.
key_finding: The landmark-derived watermark survives Deepfake edits far better than prior watermarks and enables reliable proactive detection where passive detectors collapse. Bit-wise recovery under Deepfake averaged 87.23% (128) / 87.21% (256) vs the next-best SepMark at 65.78% / 65.50%; other baselines sat near 50% (i.e., destroyed). Deepfake-detection AUC was above 95% for every one of the seven manipulations and 98.39% (128) / 98.55% (256) on a MIXED test set of all seven, whereas the strongest passive detectors (SBIs, CADDM) dropped to ~41-69% AUC on the mixed set and below 40% on some manipulations. Despite training on only SimSwap among Deepfakes, LampMark generalized to six unseen manipulations (cross-manipulation) and to an unseen dataset (LFW, ~89.73% avg recovery). A consistent structural insight: face-swapping recovery stayed >90% while face-reenactment recovery was only ~75%, attributed to reenactment wiping out background information.
core_contribution: LampMark is a proactive Deepfake-detection framework built on a novel 'landmark perceptual watermark.' Its central, distinctive idea is that the binary watermark embedded in a face image is DERIVED FROM the image's own facial-landmark geometry via a training-free pipeline (G_m), rather than being a random/model-specific bit string. The pipeline: (1) flatten facial-landmark coordinates, apply PCA to reduce them to a fixed-length vector, min-max normalize, and threshold at 0.5 to get a binary code (the 'Discrimination' property); (2) encrypt that code with a cellular-automaton system using Wolfram Rule 30 and a secret key set, XOR-ing selected keys onto the code so an attacker cannot forge a content-matched watermark (the 'Confidentiality' property); (3) train an end-to-end CNN auto-encoder (with squeeze-and-excitation + spatial attention and an adversarial discriminator) to robustly embed and recover the watermark against both benign image processing and Deepfake manipulations ('Robustness'). Detection is then framed as a consistency check: because Deepfake edits alter facial structure, the landmark-derived watermark m_sus recomputed from a suspect image will disagree with the robustly-recovered embedded watermark m_rec for fakes, but agree for benignly-processed reals. The authors claim to be the first to detect BOTH face swapping and face reenactment with a single robust watermark. (Note: prior watermark/perturbation methods such as SepMark, FaceSigns, ARWGAN, RDA, and the 'disrupting deepfakes' perturbation line are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution; LampMark's novelty is specifically the landmark-derived, cellular-automaton-encrypted watermark and the structure-consistency detection rule.)
threat_model: The protected party proactively embeds a watermark in a genuine face image BEFORE any manipulation. Adversary A performs malicious Deepfake facial manipulation (face swapping, which changes identity, or face reenactment, which changes expression/pose) on the watermarked image to create a convincing fake; the defense must still detect this because the manipulation perturbs facial landmark structure and thus breaks watermark-content consistency. Adversary B is an adaptive attacker who KNOWS the watermark-generation pipeline and tries to replace the embedded watermark with a fresh content-matched one to make a fake pass the consistency check; this is what the Rule-30 cellular-automaton encryption (with a secret key set K') is meant to defeat, since without the key the correct watermark cannot be reconstructed. A third, benign channel is modeled: normal internet redistribution degrades images via JPEG compression, noise, and blur, and the watermark must survive these so real images are NOT misclassified as fake (low false positives). Key assumption/limitation of the model: detection only works on images that were watermarked in advance by a cooperating source (proactive), and the verifier must possess the landmark pipeline and decryption keys.
limitations: (1) Proactive-only: images must be watermarked in advance by a cooperating source, so LampMark cannot flag arbitrary in-the-wild fakes that were never protected. (2) Face reenactment is the weak point: recovery accuracy for StarGAN/StyleMask/HyperReenact is only ~73-76% (128/256) vs >90% for face swapping, and the authors explicitly name cross-manipulation reenactment recovery as future work. (3) Even the headline recovery is imperfect (87% under Deepfake), leaving a gap the authors acknowledge. (4) The confidentiality argument against a pipeline-aware adaptive attacker rests on the secrecy of the cellular-automaton key set and is argued rather than empirically stress-tested with an adaptive-attack experiment in the paper. (5) Verification requires the verifier to hold the landmark pipeline and decryption keys. (6) Evaluated only on aligned face-image datasets (CelebA-HQ, LFW) at 128/256; robustness to stronger/combined distortions or adversarial removal attacks beyond the fixed benign pool is not shown.

datasets_used: CelebA-HQ (30,000 images, official train/val/test split; also used as the corpus to fit the PCA transform W)
LFW (5,749 unique identities; used for cross-dataset evaluation at 128 resolution)
quantitative_results: Visual quality (watermarked vs raw), Ours: PSNR 44.75 dB / SSIM 0.992 at 128x128; PSNR 45.45 dB / SSIM 0.995 at 256x256 (best among all baselines)
Watermark recovery under benign manipulations, Ours avg: 99.89% (128) and 99.99% (256); most challenging benign ops were GaussianNoise and Jpeg
Watermark recovery under Deepfake manipulations, Ours avg: 87.23% (128) / 87.21% (256) vs SepMark 65.78% / 65.50%; other baselines ~50%
Per-manipulation Deepfake recovery (128), Ours: SimSwap 99.95%, InfoSwap 97.99%, UniFace 99.72%, E4S 92.09%, StarGAN 73.12%, StyleMask 74.19%, HyperReenact 73.53%
Deepfake detection AUC (Ours) above 95% for all seven manipulations; mixed-set AUC 98.39% (128) / 98.55% (256)
Passive detectors on mixed set AUC: Xception 41.28%/41.42%, SBIs 60.39%/68.62%, RECCE 54.09%/52.51%, CADDM 52.04%/59.84% (128/256) — far below Ours
Cross-dataset (LFW, 128): Ours PSNR 43.14 / SSIM 0.983; benign recovery avg 99.95%; Deepfake recovery avg 83.73%; ~89.73% average recovery across all manipulations
Trained on only Jpeg(50) and SimSwap; all other benign ops and six Deepfake manipulations are unseen (cross-manipulation)
baselines_compared: Watermarking/robust frameworks: HiDDeN, MBRS, RDA, CIN, ARWGAN, SepMark
Passive Deepfake detectors: Xception, SBIs, RECCE, CADDM
Deepfake manipulation algorithms used as adversaries: SimSwap, InfoSwap, UniFace, E4S (face swapping); StarGAN, StyleMask, HyperReenact (face reenactment)
